title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Project Overview
This site documents ongoing work to migrate NOAA CoastWatch West Coast Node / SWFSC ERDDAP services and associated data processing workflows into Google Cloud Platform (GCP), while preserving existing operational data products and access patterns.
The project is part of a broader NOAA/NMFS effort to move data systems to the cloud before the end of on-premises server support. The goal is not just to “lift and shift” existing workflows, but to make them more reproducible, maintainable, and easier to operate over time.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Project goals
This documentation tracks the design, migration, and modernization of data processing systems used to support operational ocean data access.
The main goals are to:

migrate ERDDAP-supporting workflows from legacy servers to GCP
preserve scientific continuity with existing products
standardize workflow structure across projects
move toward config-driven code instead of hard-coded paths and hidden system assumptions
use reproducible infrastructure and containerized processing
document enough detail for handoff, review, and future maintenance

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Cloud architecture
The migration uses a mix of long-lived infrastructure and event-driven processing resources in GCP.
At the center of the system is a dedicated ERDDAP VM, backed by high-performance disk storage for ERDDAP cache and working directories. Cloud-hosted datasets are exposed to ERDDAP through rclone-mounted Google Cloud Storage buckets, allowing ERDDAP to serve data from GCS while still using familiar filesystem paths.
Data processing is handled through a combination of data processing VMs for interactive development and heavier operational tasks, and Cloud Run Jobs for containerized workflows that can run on a schedule. Cloud Scheduler is used to trigger recurring jobs, while Artifact Registry stores the workflow containers.
Across the system, Terraform is used to define core infrastructure, and Secret Manager is used to manage credentials and runtime secrets without storing them directly in code.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Workflow modernization
Many of the workflows documented here began as long-running operational scripts developed over many years. As part of the migration, the code has been reorganized into more standardized, reproducible, and containerized workflows suitable for cloud-based operational processing.
This includes reducing hard-coded assumptions, separating configuration from runtime logic, standardizing deployment patterns, and improving maintainability without changing the underlying science.
For examples of the common workflow structure and deployment patterns used throughout this project, see the GCP Data Processing Templates section.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Project sections
This site includes both shared infrastructure documentation and example operational cloud workflows.

Infrastructure and deployment documentation
These sections document the core infrastructure, deployment patterns, and architectural decisions used throughout the migration effort:

GCP Environment Setup
GCP Templates

These pages cover Terraform-based infrastructure, ERDDAP and Dataproc VM setup, storage and mounting strategies, container organization, deployment conventions, and other shared operational patterns used across workflows.


Operational workflow examples
These sections document end-to-end satellite processing pipelines deployed using Cloud Run Jobs and related GCP services:

VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
MUR SST processing
CHARM processing

Each workflow page includes processing overviews, deployment structure, scheduling logic, and rendered documentation for operational scripts, configuration files, templates, and containers.


Presentations and project updates
The site also includes slides and supporting material used for project presentations, architecture reviews, and migration status updates shared with collaborating groups and NOAA programs.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Status
This is a working technical record of an active migration. Some sections are polished documentation, while others are closer to implementation notes. The intent is to capture lessons learned as the system evolves, including both successful patterns and operational gotchas.
The broader objective is a cloud-based ERDDAP ecosystem that is reproducible, easier to maintain, and ready for long-term operational use.

title: GCP Environment Setup
text:
Cloud Infrastructure Setup
This chapter describes the deployment and configuration of the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) infrastructure used to support the SWFSC CoastWatch West Coast Node cloud environment.
The environment includes Terraform-managed infrastructure, ERDDAP serving systems, processing VMs, cloud storage resources, and supporting workflow components used for operational data ingest, processing, storage, and distribution. The overall architecture follows a “standardized image” and “decoupled data” model — infrastructure resources are deployed and managed through Terraform, while operational datasets remain independent from the VM itself.
This section covers the Terraform-managed infrastructure layer: virtual machines, storage buckets, networking, service accounts, IAM permissions, and supporting cloud services.

title: GCP Environment Setup
GCP Environment Setup
text:
Accessing VMs
Once infrastructure is deployed, you can connect to any VM using either the gcloud CLI or the GCP Console browser-based SSH. Both methods use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) tunneling, so no public IP address or open firewall ports are required.

Prerequisites
Before connecting to a VM for the first time, complete the following setup steps.

1. Install the Google Cloud SDK
Download and install the Google Cloud SDK for your operating system. The SDK provides the gcloud command-line tool used throughout this documentation.
After installation, initialize the SDK:
gcloud init
This walks you through selecting a Google account and default project.


2. Authenticate
Log in with your Google account:
gcloud auth login
This opens a browser window to complete authentication. Once done, confirm the active account and project:
gcloud config list


3. Confirm IAP Access
Connecting to VMs through the CLI requires the IAP-secured Tunnel User (roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor) IAM role on the project. In this setup, that role is managed by IT through a centralized roles.tf Terraform configuration — you will need to request access from whoever administers that if you don’t have it yet.
Once access has been granted, confirm the role is assigned to your account:
gcloud projects get-iam-policy YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --flatten="bindings[].members" \
    --filter="bindings.members:YOUR_EMAIL@noaa.gov" \
    --format="table(bindings.role)"
roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor should appear in the output. If it does not, follow up with your IT team or project admin.



Via the gcloud CLI
The general pattern for connecting to a VM is:
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap
Port forwarding is added via --ssh-flag to make local services on the VM accessible in your browser. The -4 flag forces IPv4, which avoids common IAP tunneling issues.
ERDDAP VM — forwards both the ERDDAP web interface (port 8080) and JupyterLab (port 8888):
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_ERDDAP_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap \
    --ssh-flag="-4 -L 8080:localhost:8080 -L 8888:localhost:8888"
Once connected, the ERDDAP interface is accessible at http://localhost:8080/erddap in your local browser (ERDDAP runs continuously via Tomcat). To use JupyterLab, start it on the VM first:
jupyter lab --no-browser --port=8888
Jupyter will print a URL with a token to the terminal — copy and paste the http://localhost:8888/lab?token=... URL into your local browser.
Dataproc / processing VM — forwards JupyterLab (port 8081 to avoid conflicts if the ERDDAP VM tunnel is already open):
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_DATAPROC_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap \
    --ssh-flag="-4 -L 8081:localhost:8081"
Once connected, start JupyterLab on the VM:
jupyter lab --no-browser --port=8081
Jupyter will print a URL with a token to the terminal — copy and paste the http://localhost:8081/lab?token=... URL into your local browser.


Via the GCP Console
For quick access without the CLI:

Go to the Compute Engine VM instances page in the GCP Console.
Find your VM in the list.
Click the SSH button in the Connect column.

The console opens a browser-based terminal connected to the VM through IAP. This is convenient for quick checks but does not support port forwarding or persistent sessions.

title: GCP Environment Setup
text:
ERDDAP VM Setup
This section describes the initialization and configuration of the dedicated ERDDAP serving environment after infrastructure has been deployed. The ERDDAP VM uses C4 high-memory instances (for example, c4-highmem-16) together with Hyperdisk Balanced storage, rclone-mounted GCS buckets for dataset access, and Tomcat-hosted ERDDAP for data distribution.

1. Configure the Operating System Environment

1.1 Configure Shared User Group Permissions
Create a shared group for operational data access and add the required users:
sudo groupadd cwatch                              # Create the cwatch group if it does not already exist
sudo usermod -aG cwatch $USER                     # Add your current user
sudo usermod -aG cwatch YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME # Add the operational/service account (e.g. erd)
newgrp cwatch                                     # Refresh group membership (or log out and back in)


1.2 Initialize and Mount the Persistent Data Disk
Before formatting or mounting the persistent data disk, determine whether the disk is new or reattached from an existing deployment. If the disk was attached from a previous VM, do not reformat it.
# Identify the attached disk
ls -l /dev/disk/by-id/

# Check whether the disk already contains a filesystem
sudo blkid /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data

# If no filesystem exists, create one
sudo mkfs.ext4 -m 0 -E lazy_itable_init=0,lazy_journal_init=0,discard \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data

# Create mount point and mount the disk
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/disks/erddap-data
sudo mount -o discard,defaults \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data \
    /mnt/disks/erddap-data

# Set permissions for the shared cwatch group
sudo chown -R root:cwatch /mnt/disks/erddap-data
sudo chmod -R 2775 /mnt/disks/erddap-data

# Optional: standard symlink used by operational workflows
sudo ln -s /mnt/disks/erddap-data /data


1.3 Configure GCS Connectivity with Rclone
Rclone is used to mount GCS buckets directly into the ERDDAP VM filesystem using a Virtual File System (VFS) mount. The persistent high-speed data disk acts as a local VFS cache to improve ERDDAP performance when serving large NetCDF datasets from cloud storage.
Install rclone:
sudo curl https://rclone.org/install.sh | sudo bash
Create a shared rclone configuration:
sudo mkdir -p /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/

sudo tee /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/rclone.conf > /dev/null <<EOF
[gcs-remote]
type = google cloud storage
env_auth = true
project_number = YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_NUMBER
location = YOUR_GCP_REGION
EOF

sudo chown -R root:cwatch /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/
Create the local cache and mount directories, then create the systemd persistence service:
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/rclone-mount.service > /dev/null <<EOF
[Unit]
Description=Rclone High-Performance GCS Mount
After=network-online.target mnt-disks-erddap\x2ddata.mount
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=notify
User=root

ExecStart=/usr/bin/rclone mount gcs-remote:YOUR_ERDDAP_BUCKET_NAME /mnt/gcs/erd \
    --config /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/rclone.conf \
    --allow-other \
    --read-only \
    --dir-cache-time 1h \
    --attr-timeout 1m \
    --vfs-cache-mode full \
    --vfs-cache-max-age 48h \
    --vfs-cache-max-size 2000G \
    --vfs-cache-poll-interval 1m \
    --cache-dir /mnt/disks/erddap-data/rclone-cache \
    --buffer-size 128M \
    --vfs-read-ahead 256M \
    --vfs-read-chunk-size 64M \
    --vfs-read-chunk-size-limit 2G \
    --dir-perms 0775 \
    --file-perms 0775 \
    --gid $(getent group cwatch | cut -d: -f3) \
    --umask 002 \
    --rc \
    --rc-no-auth

ExecStop=/usr/bin/fusermount -u /mnt/gcs/erd
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF
Key rclone mount notes:

--read-only prevents accidental modification of cloud-hosted datasets.
--vfs-cache-mode full enables local caching on the persistent disk.
--vfs-read-ahead improves sequential read performance for ERDDAP requests.
--attr-timeout 1m helps rclone detect when NRT files are replaced by science-quality versions in GCS.

Enable and start the service:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable rclone-mount.service
sudo systemctl start rclone-mount.service
Verify the mount and cache status:
ls -l /mnt/gcs/erd       # Confirm GCS bucket contents are visible
rclone rc vfs/stats       # Monitor cache usage and read-ahead status






Tip



Storage capacity: The 2 TB cache is a managed ceiling — rclone will automatically purge the oldest data if it hits this limit, ensuring the disk never fills up.
NRT updates: By checking attributes once per minute, the system will automatically fetch the updated science-quality version of a file even if the filename has not changed.





2. Install and Configure ERDDAP

2.1 Run the CoastWatch ERDDAP Installation Script
This setup uses the CoastWatch ERDDAP installation script to install Tomcat, ERDDAP, Java dependencies, and supporting system configuration.
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/erd-coastwatch/cloud-erddap-setup/main/setup_scripts/erddap_setup.sh
chmod +x erddap_setup.sh
./erddap_setup.sh

# If reinstalling, remove the previous Tomcat installation first
sudo rm -rf /usr/local/apache-tomcat-10.1.44/
./erddap_setup.sh


2.2 Configure Tomcat and ERDDAP
sudo su -s /bin/bash tomcat
cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/conf
vi server.xml

cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/content/erddap
vi setup.xml


2.3 Start and Verify ERDDAP
# Start ERDDAP
sudo su -s /bin/bash tomcat
cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/bin
./startup.sh

# Stop ERDDAP
./shutdown.sh

# Verify installation
curl http://localhost:8080

# Install additional dependencies
sudo apt install -y fontconfig
sudo apt install -y lynx

title: GCP Environment Setup
text:
Dataproc VM Setup
This section describes the initialization and configuration of processing environments used for operational workflows, testing, reprocessing, and data generation. Unlike the ERDDAP VM, which is optimized for data serving, processing VMs are intended for workflow execution, algorithm development, backfills, and validation tasks.
Processing environments typically use C4 standard instances (for example, c4-standard-16) together with Hyperdisk Balanced storage, prioritizing compute performance over the additional memory used by ERDDAP serving environments.

1. Configure the Operating System Environment

1.1 Configure Shared User Group Permissions
sudo groupadd cwatch                              # Create the cwatch group if it does not already exist
sudo usermod -aG cwatch $USER                     # Add your current user
sudo usermod -aG cwatch YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME # Add the operational/service account (e.g. erd)
newgrp cwatch                                     # Refresh group membership (or log out and back in)


1.2 Initialize and Mount Persistent Storage
ls -l /dev/disk/by-id/
sudo blkid /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data

sudo mkfs.ext4 -m 0 -E lazy_itable_init=0,lazy_journal_init=0,discard \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data

sudo mkdir -p /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo mount -o discard,defaults \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data \
    /mnt/disks/processing-data

sudo chown -R root:cwatch /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo chmod -R 2775 /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo ln -s /mnt/disks/processing-data /data



2. Configure the Data Processing Environment

2.1 Install Conda Package Manager
curl -O https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
conda config --add channels conda-forge

# Create and activate a project environment
conda create --name ENV_NAME
conda activate ENV_NAME

# Install common scientific and workflow dependencies
conda install numpy pandas netCDF4 python-dateutil
conda install -c conda-forge nco
conda install -c conda-forge podaac-data-subscriber

# Clean up
rm Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh


2.2 Configure NASA Earthdata Authentication
Many satellite workflows require NASA Earthdata authentication. Create a .netrc file in the user home directory:
echo "machine urs.earthdata.nasa.gov login YOUR_EARTHDATA_USERNAME password YOUR_EARTHDATA_PASSWORD" >> ~/.netrc
chmod 600 ~/.netrc






Warning



Do not commit .netrc, cookies, passwords, or credentials to Git repositories.




2.3 Test External Data Access
This example uses MUR4.2 as a test dataset — the files are small and the workflow validates NASA authentication, external data access, local storage, and the processing environment.
mkdir -p /data/podaac/data

podaac-data-downloader \
    -c MUR25-JPL-L4-GLOB-v04.2 \
    -d /data/podaac/data \
    --start-date 2025-08-01T21:00:00Z \
    --end-date 2025-08-10T21:00:00Z


2.4 Install External Processing Software
Some workflows require additional compiled tools. Two common examples:
DINEOF — for gap-filling satellite data:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y gfortran make libarpack2-dev libnetcdf-dev libnetcdff-dev git

git clone https://github.com/Aida-Alvera/DINEOF
cd DINEOF/
cp config.mk.template config.mk
make
CoastWatch Utilities (cwutils) — download the appropriate Linux package from the CoastWatch Utilities distribution site and transfer it to the processing VM:
# Transfer from local machine
gcloud compute scp \
    /path/to/cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz \
    YOUR_USERNAME@YOUR_PROCESSING_VM_NAME:~/

# Unpack on the VM
tar -zxf cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
rm cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz



3. Configure Project Workspace
Processing VMs maintain one directory per workflow or project using a standardized template that provides a consistent structure across workflows. See the GCP Data Processing Templates page for the full template reference.
Project template repository: erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template
cd ~
git clone https://github.com/erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template.git
cp -R vm-dataproc-template YOUR_PROJECT_NAME   # Example: viirs_netpp
cd YOUR_PROJECT_NAME
Create a persistent storage directory for the project and replace the template data directory with a symlink:
mkdir -p /data/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NAME
rm -rf data
ln -s /data/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NAME data
This keeps large datasets, temporary products, downloads, and workflow outputs separated from the VM boot disk while maintaining a lightweight project workspace. Code, configuration, notebooks, templates, and documentation remain in the user workspace; the data/ directory points to persistent external storage on the mounted Hyperdisk.

title: GCP Environment Setup
text:
Migration, Upgrade, and Recovery

Optional: Data Preservation Snapshot






Note



Required only when rebuilding, migrating, or cloning an existing VM rather than deploying from a fresh image — for example, during machine family changes or environment rebuilds where the existing data and configuration need to be preserved.


Before creating a snapshot, stop any active services writing to the disk to ensure filesystem consistency:
sudo systemctl stop tomcat
Create a persistent disk snapshot from Cloud Shell or another authenticated terminal:
gcloud compute disks snapshot YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --snapshot-names=YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME-migration-backup


Optional: Migration Snapshot Cleanup






Note



Required only when rebuilding or migrating an existing VM using temporary manual snapshots. In many environments, temporary migration snapshots are retained for at least 24 hours before cleanup.


After the new environment has been validated and operating normally, delete the migration snapshot:
gcloud compute snapshots delete YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME-migration-backup --quiet

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
Template Repositories
To support consistent, reproducible workflows across projects, SWFSC CoastWatch maintains two GitHub template repositories. These templates provide a standardized starting point for new data processing projects deployed on Google Cloud Platform.

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
Cloud Run Template
Repository: erd-coastwatch/cloud-run-template
Use this template for containerized batch processing jobs deployed on Google Cloud Run. It follows the same structure used by the VIIRS NetPP, MUR SST, and CHARM workflows and is designed for jobs that run on a schedule or on demand, process data, and write results to GCS.
cloud-run-template/
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml          # Runtime settings (sensors, GCS paths, API keys)
│   └── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, push, and deploy the Cloud Run job
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Job mode selector
├── scripts/                # Processing and orchestration logic
└── templates/              # CDL or other output templates


Dataproc / VM Template
Repository: erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template
Use this template for interactive or long-running workflows that run directly on a Dataproc or processing VM. It is structured for projects that combine notebooks, scripts, and reusable Python modules, with access to GCS-mounted data.
vm-dataproc-template/
├── README.md               # Project overview and setup instructions
├── environment.yml         # Conda environment
├── config.yml              # Configuration file
├── LICENSE
├── notebooks/              # Jupyter notebooks for exploration and analysis
├── scripts/                # Shell and Python workflow scripts
├── src/                    # Reusable Python modules and helpers
├── data/                   # Symlink to persistent storage on the mounted processing disk
├── templates/              # CDL or CSV template files
├── resources/              # Misc reference files used during processing
├── logs/                   # Runtime logs
├── docs/                   # Documentation (Quarto or Sphinx)
└── tests/                  # Unit tests
GCS mount points follow the convention:
/mnt/gcs/data/<project>/           # Large input data
/mnt/gcs/erddap_data_pw/<project>/ # ERDDAP-served output data
The data/ directory in the template is a symlink to project-specific persistent storage on the mounted Hyperdisk, keeping large datasets and workflow outputs separated from the VM boot disk. See the GCP Environment Setup page for the full workspace initialization steps.
Periodically review the template repository for updates to the standard project structure.

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
How to Use a Template

Go to the template repo on GitHub and click “Use this template” → “Create a new repository”.
Name your repo following the project naming convention (e.g. viirs-netpp, seaice-sst).
Clone your new repo to your local machine or VM:

git clone https://github.com/erd-coastwatch/YOUR_NEW_REPO.git
cd YOUR_NEW_REPO

Fill in config.yml with your project-specific settings — sensor names, GCS bucket paths, date ranges, and any API parameters.
Add your scripts and notebooks to the appropriate directories.
For Cloud Run projects, build and deploy using deploy_job.sh once your Dockerfile and scripts are ready.







Note



These templates are intentionally minimal — they provide structure, not implementation. Each project fills in the logic specific to its data source and processing requirements.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for retrieving and distributing MODIS-Aqua Level-3 satellite products — ocean color, SST, particulate organic carbon (POC), and particulate inorganic carbon (PIC) — for both near-real-time (NRT) and science quality (delayed) streams.
The workflow queries the NASA OceanColor file search API, compares results against what is already staged in the production GCS bucket, and downloads only the missing files. Each file is then published to GCS via send_to_servers() and removed from local disk.
Products retrieved and published:
All products are produced for three temporal composites: 1-day, 8-day, and monthly.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
MH1 Ocean Color (NRT + Science) — chlorophyll-a, nFLH, PAR, Kd490
MH1 SST (NRT + Science) — sea surface temperature; also generates a masked variant (sstMasked) where qual_sst >= 0
MPOC (NRT + Science) — particulate organic carbon
MPIC (NRT + Science) — particulate inorganic carbon

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Repository structure
mh1-mpoc-mpic-processor/
├── config.yml              # Runtime settings (GCS paths, NASA endpoints)
├── requirements.txt        # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, push, and deploy the Cloud Run job
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Job entrypoint; runs all NRT then all Science suites
├── scripts/
│   ├── MH1/
│   │   ├── getMH1OceanColor_NRT.py   # Ocean color NRT retrieval
│   │   ├── getMH1OceanColor_Sci.py   # Ocean color science quality retrieval
│   │   ├── getMH1SST_NRT.py          # SST NRT retrieval + masking
│   │   └── getMH1SST_Sci.py          # SST science quality retrieval + masking
│   ├── MPIC/
│   │   ├── getMPIC_NRT.py            # PIC NRT retrieval
│   │   └── getMPIC_Sci.py            # PIC science quality retrieval
│   └── MPOC/
│       ├── getMPOC_NRT.py            # POC NRT retrieval
│       └── getMPOC_Sci.py            # POC science quality retrieval
└── src/
    ├── __init__.py
    └── roylib.py                     # Shared GCS and processing utilities

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Cloud Run job
The workflow runs as a single Cloud Run job scheduled daily. entrypoint.sh drives the full suite in sequence: NRT suites first, then science quality suites.








Suite
Scripts
Description




MH1 NRT
getMH1OceanColor_NRT.py, getMH1SST_NRT.py
Ocean color + SST near-real-time


MH1 Science
getMH1OceanColor_Sci.py, getMH1SST_Sci.py
Ocean color + SST science quality


MPIC NRT
getMPIC_NRT.py
PIC near-real-time


MPIC Science
getMPIC_Sci.py
PIC science quality


MPOC NRT
getMPOC_NRT.py
POC near-real-time


MPOC Science
getMPOC_Sci.py
POC science quality

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Processing pipeline
Each suite follows the same pattern:
Cloud Scheduler → Cloud Run Job → Query NASA API → Check GCS → Download missing files → Upload to GCS → Cleanup
The NASA OceanColor file_search API is queried with a date range spanning the current calendar year. The resulting filename list is compared against the production bucket inventory from list_bucket_content(). Only files absent from GCS are downloaded via wget using Earthdata credentials injected at runtime from Secret Manager.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
SST masking
For both NRT and science SST products, the workflow generates a second masked variant alongside the raw download. The mask is applied by setting SST values to fill where qual_sst < 0, creating a sstMasked variable. Both the raw and masked files are uploaded to separate GCS paths and removed from local disk.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
NASA API dataset IDs



Dataset
dtid
Notes




MH1 Ocean Color NRT
1055
MODIS-Aqua L3m NRT


MH1 Ocean Color Science
1043
MODIS-Aqua L3m Standard Science


MH1 SST NRT
1061
MODIS-Aqua SST L3m NRT


MH1 SST Science
1049
MODIS-Aqua SST L3m Standard Science


MPOC / MPIC NRT
1055
Shared with ocean color NRT stream


MPOC / MPIC Science
1102
Standard Science Quality

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
GCS path conventions
NRT products are published under satellite/<DATASET>_NRT/<product>/<period>/ and science quality products under satellite/<DATASET>/<product>/<period>/. For example:
satellite/MH1_NRT/chla/1day/
satellite/MH1/chla/1day/
satellite/MH1_NRT/sst/mday/
satellite/MH1/sstMask/8day/
satellite/MPOC_NRT/1day/
satellite/MPIC/mday/

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Credentials and secrets
Earthdata Login credentials (netrc and urs_cookies) are injected at runtime via Google Secret Manager and copied to writable /tmp paths. The config file is also mounted from Secret Manager and its path is exported as ROYLIB_CONFIG, which roylib.py uses to load all runtime settings.

title: VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating daily and monthly VIIRS net primary productivity products.
The workflow computes daily and monthly satellite-derived primary productivity products from VIIRS ocean color inputs using a containerized Cloud Run Job pattern.
Products are supported for:

title: VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
text:
NOAA-20 VIIRS - daily and monthly productivity products
SNPP VIIRS - daily and monthly productivity products

title: VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
text:
Repository structure
viirs-netpp/
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml          # Runtime settings
│   └── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, push, and deploy Cloud Run jobs
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Job mode selector
├── scripts/
│   ├── control_viirs_netpp.py          # Daily workflow controller
│   ├── make_viirs_netpp.py             # Daily productivity processor
│   ├── control_viirs_netpp_monthly.py  # Monthly workflow controller
│   └── make_viirs_netpp_monthly.py     # Monthly productivity processor
└── templates/
    ├── nasa_noaa20_4km.cdl        # NOAA-20 daily template
    ├── nasa_noaa20_4km_month.cdl  # NOAA-20 monthly template
    ├── nasa_snpp_4km.cdl          # SNPP daily template
    └── nasa_snpp_4km_month.cdl    # SNPP monthly template

title: VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
text:
Processing pipeline
Each daily run follows this general sequence:
Cloud Scheduler → Cloud Run Job → Download inputs → Compute productivity → Apply template metadata → Publish to GCS → ERDDAP ingestion
The daily controller handles date selection, sensor configuration, input discovery, and calls the product generation script for each target date.

title: VIIRS Net Primary Productivity
text:
Monthly composites
Monthly processing is triggered through the monthly job mode.
The monthly workflow gathers the required daily productivity files, builds a monthly composite, applies the appropriate CDL template, and publishes the final NetCDF output for ERDDAP.

title: MUR SST
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating daily and monthly MUR Sea Surface Temperature products from NASA’s Multi-scale Ultra-high Resolution (MUR) SST analysis.
The workflow ingests MUR SST data from NASA PO.DAAC, computes daily anomalies and frontal edge detections, and generates monthly composites. It handles the full NRT → Final upgrade cycle automatically, detecting when NASA has promoted a near-real-time granule to science quality and re-downloading accordingly.
Two MUR versions are supported:

title: MUR SST
text:
MUR v4.1 - full processing: download, anomaly computation, fronts detection, and monthly composites
MUR v4.2 - download only (smart sync with NRT → Final upgrade detection)

title: MUR SST
text:
Repository structure
mur-daily-workflows/
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml          # Runtime settings (GCS paths, collection names, intervals)
│   └── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, push, and deploy Cloud Run jobs
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Job mode selector
├── src/
│   └── roylib.py           # Shared internal utility library
├── scripts/
│   ├── MURanom1day.py                  # Daily anomaly computation (MUR v4.1)
│   ├── mur_v41_downloader_dailyproc.sh # MUR v4.1 smart sync + processing
│   ├── mur_v42_downloader.sh           # MUR v4.2 smart sync (download only)
│   ├── fronts/
│   │   ├── calc_mur_fronts.py          # Fronts runner (WC + ATL regions)
│   │   ├── canny_lib.py                # Unified Canny edge detection library
│   │   ├── Canny1.py                   # WC region Canny implementation
│   │   └── Canny2.py                   # ATL region Canny implementation
│   └── MonthlyProc/
│       ├── MUR41_MonProc.sh            # Monthly processing runner
│       ├── CompMURmon.py               # Monthly SST composite
│       └── CompMurAnomMon.py           # Monthly anomaly composite
└── templates/
    ├── murAnomTemplate.nc      # Template for daily anomaly output
    ├── murSSTmday.nc           # Template for monthly SST composite
    └── murSSTmdayAnom.nc       # Template for monthly anomaly composite

title: MUR SST
text:
Cloud Run jobs
The workflow runs as three separate Cloud Run jobs, all built from the same container image and dispatched via JOB_MODE in entrypoint.sh:








Job mode
Script
Description




mur41_daily
mur_v41_downloader_dailyproc.sh
MUR v4.1 download + anomaly + fronts


mur42_daily
mur_v42_downloader.sh
MUR v4.2 download only


mur41_monthly
MUR41_MonProc.sh
Monthly SST and anomaly composites

title: MUR SST
text:
Processing pipeline (MUR v4.1 daily)
Each day in the lookback window goes through the following stages:
GCS probe → NASA stage check → Download (PO.DAAC) → Anomaly → Fronts (WC + ATL) → Publish
NRT → Final upgrade logic: The downloader checks both the GCS copy (via GDAL metadata) and the NASA CMR/OPeNDAP metadata to detect when a near-real-time file has been promoted to science quality. If GCS has NRT and NASA has Final, the file is re-downloaded and reprocessed automatically.

title: MUR SST
text:
Fronts detection
Daily SST frontal edges are computed for two regional subsets using the Canny edge detection algorithm:



Region
Coverage




West Coast (WC)
22°N-51°N, 135°W-105°W


East Coast (ATL)
20°N-50°N, 90°W-60°W



The WC and ATL regions use slightly different Canny implementations (Canny1 and Canny2) which are unified through canny_lib.py. Output includes frontal edges, x/y gradients, and gradient magnitude in NetCDF format.

title: MUR SST
text:
Monthly composites
Monthly processing is triggered by JOB_MODE=mur41_monthly and runs two compositors in sequence:

CompMURmon.py - averages all daily SST files for the target month into a single monthly mean, using parallel GCS downloads and chunked stripe accumulation to stay within container memory limits.
CompMurAnomMon.py - averages all daily anomaly files for the target month into a monthly anomaly composite using the same pattern.

The target month is controlled by the MONTH_OFFSET environment variable (default: 1 = last completed month).

title: CHARM

Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating CHARM products and derived habitat prediction datasets.
The workflow ingests VIIRS ocean color inputs, applies DINEOF gap-filling and CHARM model logic, and generates gridded bloom prediction products for ERDDAP publication.
The system includes:
